| DROP TABLE |     |     |     |     |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| [Prev](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/18/sql-dropsubscription.html "DROP SUBSCRIPTION") | [Up](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/18/sql-commands.html "SQL Commands") | SQL Commands | [Home](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/18/index.html "PostgreSQL 18.0 Documentation") | [Next](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/18/sql-droptablespace.html "DROP TABLESPACE") |

* * *

DROP TABLE
----------

DROP TABLE — remove a table

Synopsis
--------

DROP TABLE \[ IF EXISTS \] _`name`_ \[, ...\] \[ CASCADE | RESTRICT \]

Description
-----------

`DROP TABLE` removes tables from the database. Only the table owner, the schema owner, and superuser can drop a table. To empty a table of rows without destroying the table, use [`DELETE`](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/18/sql-delete.html "DELETE")
 or [`TRUNCATE`](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/18/sql-truncate.html "TRUNCATE")
.

`DROP TABLE` always removes any indexes, rules, triggers, and constraints that exist for the target table. However, to drop a table that is referenced by a view or a foreign-key constraint of another table, `CASCADE` must be specified. (`CASCADE` will remove a dependent view entirely, but in the foreign-key case it will only remove the foreign-key constraint, not the other table entirely.)

Parameters
----------

`IF EXISTS`

Do not throw an error if the table does not exist. A notice is issued in this case.

_`name`_

The name (optionally schema-qualified) of the table to drop.

`CASCADE`

Automatically drop objects that depend on the table (such as views), and in turn all objects that depend on those objects (see [Section 5.15](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/18/ddl-depend.html "5.15. Dependency Tracking")
).

`RESTRICT`

Refuse to drop the table if any objects depend on it. This is the default.

Examples
--------

To destroy two tables, `films` and `distributors`:

DROP TABLE films, distributors;

Compatibility
-------------

This command conforms to the SQL standard, except that the standard only allows one table to be dropped per command, and apart from the `IF EXISTS` option, which is a PostgreSQL extension.

See Also
--------

[ALTER TABLE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/18/sql-altertable.html "ALTER TABLE")
, [CREATE TABLE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/18/sql-createtable.html "CREATE TABLE")

* * *

| DROP SUBSCRIPTION | [Home](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/18/index.html "PostgreSQL 18.0 Documentation") | DROP TABLESPACE |

Chapter 13. Concurrency Control
===============================

Table of Contents

13.1. [Introduction](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.6/mvcc-intro.html)

13.2. [Transaction Isolation](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.6/transaction-iso.html)

13.2.1. [Read Committed Isolation Level](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.6/transaction-iso.html#XACT-READ-COMMITTED)

13.2.2. [Repeatable Read Isolation Level](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.6/transaction-iso.html#XACT-REPEATABLE-READ)

13.2.3. [Serializable Isolation Level](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.6/transaction-iso.html#XACT-SERIALIZABLE)

13.3. [Explicit Locking](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.6/explicit-locking.html)

13.3.1. [Table-level Locks](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.6/explicit-locking.html#LOCKING-TABLES)

13.3.2. [Row-level Locks](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.6/explicit-locking.html#LOCKING-ROWS)

13.3.3. [Page-level Locks](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.6/explicit-locking.html#LOCKING-PAGES)

13.3.4. [Deadlocks](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.6/explicit-locking.html#LOCKING-DEADLOCKS)

13.3.5. [Advisory Locks](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.6/explicit-locking.html#ADVISORY-LOCKS)

13.4. [Data Consistency Checks at the Application Level](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.6/applevel-consistency.html)

13.4.1. [Enforcing Consistency With Serializable Transactions](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.6/applevel-consistency.html#SERIALIZABLE-CONSISTENCY)

13.4.2. [Enforcing Consistency With Explicit Blocking Locks](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.6/applevel-consistency.html#NON-SERIALIZABLE-CONSISTENCY)

13.5. [Caveats](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.6/mvcc-caveats.html)

13.6. [Locking and Indexes](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.6/locking-indexes.html)

This chapter describes the behavior of the PostgreSQL database system when two or more sessions try to access the same data at the same time. The goals in that situation are to allow efficient access for all sessions while maintaining strict data integrity. Every developer of database applications should be familiar with the topics covered in this chapter.

* * *

Chapter 23. Routine Database Maintenance Tasks
==============================================

Table of Contents

23.1. [Routine Vacuuming](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.3/routine-vacuuming.html)

23.1.1. [Recovering Disk Space](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.3/routine-vacuuming.html#VACUUM-FOR-SPACE-RECOVERY)

23.1.2. [Updating Planner Statistics](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.3/routine-vacuuming.html#VACUUM-FOR-STATISTICS)

23.1.3. [Preventing Transaction ID Wraparound Failures](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.3/routine-vacuuming.html#VACUUM-FOR-WRAPAROUND)

23.1.4. [The Auto-Vacuum Daemon](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.3/routine-vacuuming.html#AUTOVACUUM)

23.2. [Routine Reindexing](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.3/routine-reindex.html)

23.3. [Log File Maintenance](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.3/logfile-maintenance.html)

PostgreSQL, like any database software, requires that certain tasks be performed regularly to achieve optimum performance. The tasks discussed here are required, but they are repetitive in nature and can easily be automated using standard Unix tools such as cron scripts or Windows' Task Scheduler. But it is the database administrator's responsibility to set up appropriate scripts, and to check that they execute successfully.

One obvious maintenance task is creation of backup copies of the data on a regular schedule. Without a recent backup, you have no chance of recovery after a catastrophe (disk failure, fire, mistakenly dropping a critical table, etc.). The backup and recovery mechanisms available in PostgreSQL are discussed at length in [Chapter 24](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.3/backup.html)
.

The other main category of maintenance task is periodic "vacuuming" of the database. This activity is discussed in [Section 23.1](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.3/routine-vacuuming.html)
. Closely related to this is updating the statistics that will be used by the query planner, as discussed in [Section 23.1.2](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.3/routine-vacuuming.html#VACUUM-FOR-STATISTICS)
.

Another task that might need periodic attention is log file management. This is discussed in [Section 23.3](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.3/logfile-maintenance.html)
.

PostgreSQL is low-maintenance compared to some other database management systems. Nonetheless, appropriate attention to these tasks will go far towards ensuring a pleasant and productive experience with the system.

* * *

| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| [Prev](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/catalog-pg-ts-template.html "52.63. pg_ts_template") | [Up](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/catalogs.html "Chapter 52. System Catalogs") | Chapter 52. System Catalogs | [Home](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/index.html "PostgreSQL 18.0 Documentation") | [Next](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/catalog-pg-user-mapping.html "52.65. pg_user_mapping") |

* * *

52.64. `pg_type` [#](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/catalog-pg-type.html#CATALOG-PG-TYPE)

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The catalog `pg_type` stores information about data types. Base types and enum types (scalar types) are created with [`CREATE TYPE`](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/sql-createtype.html "CREATE TYPE")
, and domains with [`CREATE DOMAIN`](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/sql-createdomain.html "CREATE DOMAIN")
. A composite type is automatically created for each table in the database, to represent the row structure of the table. It is also possible to create composite types with `CREATE TYPE AS`.

**Table 52.64. `pg_type` Columns**

| Column Type<br><br>Description |
| --- |
| `oid` `oid`<br><br>Row identifier |
| `typname` `name`<br><br>Data type name |
| `typnamespace` `oid` (references [`pg_namespace`](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/catalog-pg-namespace.html "52.32. pg_namespace")<br>.`oid`)<br><br>The OID of the namespace that contains this type |
| `typowner` `oid` (references [`pg_authid`](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/catalog-pg-authid.html "52.8. pg_authid")<br>.`oid`)<br><br>Owner of the type |
| `typlen` `int2`<br><br>For a fixed-size type, `typlen` is the number of bytes in the internal representation of the type. But for a variable-length type, `typlen` is negative. -1 indicates a “varlena” type (one that has a length word), -2 indicates a null-terminated C string. |
| `typbyval` `bool`<br><br>`typbyval` determines whether internal routines pass a value of this type by value or by reference. `typbyval` had better be false if `typlen` is not 1, 2, or 4 (or 8 on machines where Datum is 8 bytes). Variable-length types are always passed by reference. Note that `typbyval` can be false even if the length would allow pass-by-value. |
| `typtype` `char`<br><br>`typtype` is `b` for a base type, `c` for a composite type (e.g., a table's row type), `d` for a domain, `e` for an enum type, `p` for a pseudo-type, `r` for a range type, or `m` for a multirange type. See also `typrelid` and `typbasetype`. |
| `typcategory` `char`<br><br>`typcategory` is an arbitrary classification of data types that is used by the parser to determine which implicit casts should be “preferred”. See [Table 52.65](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/catalog-pg-type.html#CATALOG-TYPCATEGORY-TABLE "Table 52.65. typcategory Codes")<br>. |
| `typispreferred` `bool`<br><br>True if the type is a preferred cast target within its `typcategory` |
| `typisdefined` `bool`<br><br>True if the type is defined, false if this is a placeholder entry for a not-yet-defined type. When `typisdefined` is false, nothing except the type name, namespace, and OID can be relied on. |
| `typdelim` `char`<br><br>Character that separates two values of this type when parsing array input. Note that the delimiter is associated with the array element data type, not the array data type. |
| `typrelid` `oid` (references [`pg_class`](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/catalog-pg-class.html "52.11. pg_class")<br>.`oid`)<br><br>If this is a composite type (see `typtype`), then this column points to the [`pg_class`](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/catalog-pg-class.html "52.11. pg_class")<br> entry that defines the corresponding table. (For a free-standing composite type, the [`pg_class`](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/catalog-pg-class.html "52.11. pg_class")<br> entry doesn't really represent a table, but it is needed anyway for the type's [`pg_attribute`](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/catalog-pg-attribute.html "52.7. pg_attribute")<br> entries to link to.) Zero for non-composite types. |
| `typsubscript` `regproc` (references [`pg_proc`](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/catalog-pg-proc.html "52.39. pg_proc")<br>.`oid`)<br><br>Subscripting handler function's OID, or zero if this type doesn't support subscripting. Types that are “true” array types have `typsubscript` = `array_subscript_handler`, but other types may have other handler functions to implement specialized subscripting behavior. |
| `typelem` `oid` (references [`pg_type`](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/catalog-pg-type.html "52.64. pg_type")<br>.`oid`)<br><br>If `typelem` is not zero then it identifies another row in `pg_type`, defining the type yielded by subscripting. This should be zero if `typsubscript` is zero. However, it can be zero when `typsubscript` isn't zero, if the handler doesn't need `typelem` to determine the subscripting result type. Note that a `typelem` dependency is considered to imply physical containment of the element type in this type; so DDL changes on the element type might be restricted by the presence of this type. |
| `typarray` `oid` (references [`pg_type`](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/catalog-pg-type.html "52.64. pg_type")<br>.`oid`)<br><br>If `typarray` is not zero then it identifies another row in `pg_type`, which is the “true” array type having this type as element |
| `typinput` `regproc` (references [`pg_proc`](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/catalog-pg-proc.html "52.39. pg_proc")<br>.`oid`)<br><br>Input conversion function (text format) |
| `typoutput` `regproc` (references [`pg_proc`](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/catalog-pg-proc.html "52.39. pg_proc")<br>.`oid`)<br><br>Output conversion function (text format) |
| `typreceive` `regproc` (references [`pg_proc`](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/catalog-pg-proc.html "52.39. pg_proc")<br>.`oid`)<br><br>Input conversion function (binary format), or zero if none |
| `typsend` `regproc` (references [`pg_proc`](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/catalog-pg-proc.html "52.39. pg_proc")<br>.`oid`)<br><br>Output conversion function (binary format), or zero if none |
| `typmodin` `regproc` (references [`pg_proc`](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/catalog-pg-proc.html "52.39. pg_proc")<br>.`oid`)<br><br>Type modifier input function, or zero if type does not support modifiers |
| `typmodout` `regproc` (references [`pg_proc`](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/catalog-pg-proc.html "52.39. pg_proc")<br>.`oid`)<br><br>Type modifier output function, or zero to use the standard format |
| `typanalyze` `regproc` (references [`pg_proc`](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/catalog-pg-proc.html "52.39. pg_proc")<br>.`oid`)<br><br>Custom [ANALYZE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/sql-analyze.html "ANALYZE")<br> function, or zero to use the standard function |
| `typalign` `char`<br><br>`typalign` is the alignment required when storing a value of this type. It applies to storage on disk as well as most representations of the value inside PostgreSQL. When multiple values are stored consecutively, such as in the representation of a complete row on disk, padding is inserted before a datum of this type so that it begins on the specified boundary. The alignment reference is the beginning of the first datum in the sequence. Possible values are:<br><br>*   `c` = `char` alignment, i.e., no alignment needed.<br>    <br>*   `s` = `short` alignment (2 bytes on most machines).<br>    <br>*   `i` = `int` alignment (4 bytes on most machines).<br>    <br>*   `d` = `double` alignment (8 bytes on many machines, but by no means all). |
| `typstorage` `char`<br><br>`typstorage` tells for varlena types (those with `typlen` = -1) if the type is prepared for toasting and what the default strategy for attributes of this type should be. Possible values are:<br><br>*   `p` (plain): Values must always be stored plain (non-varlena types always use this value).<br>    <br>*   `e` (external): Values can be stored in a secondary “TOAST” relation (if relation has one, see `pg_class.reltoastrelid`).<br>    <br>*   `m` (main): Values can be compressed and stored inline.<br>    <br>*   `x` (extended): Values can be compressed and/or moved to a secondary relation.<br>    <br><br>`x` is the usual choice for toast-able types. Note that `m` values can also be moved out to secondary storage, but only as a last resort (`e` and `x` values are moved first). |
| `typnotnull` `bool`<br><br>`typnotnull` represents a not-null constraint on a type. Used for domains only. |
| `typbasetype` `oid` (references [`pg_type`](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/catalog-pg-type.html "52.64. pg_type")<br>.`oid`)<br><br>If this is a domain (see `typtype`), then `typbasetype` identifies the type that this one is based on. Zero if this type is not a domain. |
| `typtypmod` `int4`<br><br>Domains use `typtypmod` to record the `typmod` to be applied to their base type (-1 if base type does not use a `typmod`). -1 if this type is not a domain. |
| `typndims` `int4`<br><br>`typndims` is the number of array dimensions for a domain over an array (that is, `typbasetype` is an array type). Zero for types other than domains over array types. |
| `typcollation` `oid` (references [`pg_collation`](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/catalog-pg-collation.html "52.12. pg_collation")<br>.`oid`)<br><br>`typcollation` specifies the collation of the type. If the type does not support collations, this will be zero. A base type that supports collations will have a nonzero value here, typically `DEFAULT_COLLATION_OID`. A domain over a collatable type can have a collation OID different from its base type's, if one was specified for the domain. |
| `typdefaultbin` `pg_node_tree`<br><br>If `typdefaultbin` is not null, it is the `nodeToString()` representation of a default expression for the type. This is only used for domains. |
| `typdefault` `text`<br><br>`typdefault` is null if the type has no associated default value. If `typdefaultbin` is not null, `typdefault` must contain a human-readable version of the default expression represented by `typdefaultbin`. If `typdefaultbin` is null and `typdefault` is not, then `typdefault` is the external representation of the type's default value, which can be fed to the type's input converter to produce a constant. |
| `typacl` `aclitem[]`<br><br>Access privileges; see [Section 5.8](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/ddl-priv.html "5.8. Privileges")<br> for details |

  

### Note

For fixed-width types used in system tables, it is critical that the size and alignment defined in `pg_type` agree with the way that the compiler will lay out the column in a structure representing a table row.

[Table 52.65](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/catalog-pg-type.html#CATALOG-TYPCATEGORY-TABLE "Table 52.65. typcategory Codes")
 lists the system-defined values of `typcategory`. Any future additions to this list will also be upper-case ASCII letters. All other ASCII characters are reserved for user-defined categories.

**Table 52.65. `typcategory` Codes**

 
| Code | Category |
| --- | --- |
| `A` | Array types |
| `B` | Boolean types |
| `C` | Composite types |
| `D` | Date/time types |
| `E` | Enum types |
| `G` | Geometric types |
| `I` | Network address types |
| `N` | Numeric types |
| `P` | Pseudo-types |
| `R` | Range types |
| `S` | String types |
| `T` | Timespan types |
| `U` | User-defined types |
| `V` | Bit-string types |
| `X` | `unknown` type |
| `Z` | Internal-use types |

  

* * *

|     |     |     |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [Prev](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/catalog-pg-ts-template.html "52.63. pg_ts_template") | [Up](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/catalogs.html "Chapter 52. System Catalogs") | [Next](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/catalog-pg-user-mapping.html "52.65. pg_user_mapping") |
| 52.63. `pg_ts_template` | [Home](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/index.html "PostgreSQL 18.0 Documentation") | 52.65. `pg_user_mapping` |

Submit correction
-----------------

If you see anything in the documentation that is not correct, does not match your experience with the particular feature or requires further clarification, please use [this form](https://www.postgresql.org/account/comments/new/18/catalog-pg-type.html/)
 to report a documentation issue.

You may want to view the same page for the [current](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/sql-commands.html "PostgreSQL  - SQL Commands")
 version, or one of the other supported versions listed above instead.

| PostgreSQL 7.2.8 Documentation |     |     |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [Prev](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/reference-preface.html) |     | [Next](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-abort.html) |

* * *

I. SQL Commands
===============

This part contains reference information for the SQL commands supported by PostgreSQL. By "SQL" the language in general is meant; information about the standards conformance and compatibility of each command can be found on the respective reference page.

Table of Contents

[ABORT](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-abort.html)
 --  abort the current transaction

[ALTER GROUP](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-altergroup.html)
 --  add users to a group or remove users from a group

[ALTER TABLE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-altertable.html)
 --  change the definition of a table

[ALTER USER](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-alteruser.html)
 --  change a database user account

[ANALYZE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-analyze.html)
 --  collect statistics about a database

[BEGIN](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-begin.html)
 --  start a transaction block

[CHECKPOINT](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-checkpoint.html)
 -- force a transaction log checkpoint

[CLOSE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-close.html)
 --  close a cursor

[CLUSTER](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-cluster.html)
 --  cluster a table according to an index

[COMMENT](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-comment.html)
 --  define or change the comment of an object

[COMMIT](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-commit.html)
 --  commit the current transaction

[COPY](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-copy.html)
 --  copy data between files and tables

[CREATE AGGREGATE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-createaggregate.html)
 --  define a new aggregate function

[CREATE CONSTRAINT TRIGGER](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-createconstraint.html)
 --  define a new constraint trigger

[CREATE DATABASE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-createdatabase.html)
 --  create a new database

[CREATE FUNCTION](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-createfunction.html)
 -- define a new function

[CREATE GROUP](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-creategroup.html)
 --  define a new user group

[CREATE INDEX](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-createindex.html)
 --  define a new index

[CREATE LANGUAGE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-createlanguage.html)
 -- define a new procedural language

[CREATE OPERATOR](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-createoperator.html)
 --  define a new operator

[CREATE RULE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-createrule.html)
 --  define a new rewrite rule

[CREATE SEQUENCE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-createsequence.html)
 --  define a new sequence generator

[CREATE TABLE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-createtable.html)
 -- define a new table

[CREATE TABLE AS](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-createtableas.html)
 -- create a new table from the results of a query

[CREATE TRIGGER](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-createtrigger.html)
 --  define a new trigger

[CREATE TYPE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-createtype.html)
 --  define a new data type

[CREATE USER](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-createuser.html)
 --  define a new database user account

[CREATE VIEW](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-createview.html)
 --  define a new view

[DECLARE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-declare.html)
 --  define a cursor

[DELETE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-delete.html)
 --  delete rows of a table

[DROP AGGREGATE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-dropaggregate.html)
 --  remove a user-defined aggregate function

[DROP DATABASE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-dropdatabase.html)
 --  remove a database

[DROP FUNCTION](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-dropfunction.html)
 --  remove a user-defined function

[DROP GROUP](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-dropgroup.html)
 --  remove a user group

[DROP INDEX](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-dropindex.html)
 --  remove an index

[DROP LANGUAGE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-droplanguage.html)
 --  remove a user-defined procedural language

[DROP OPERATOR](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-dropoperator.html)
 --  remove a user-defined operator

[DROP RULE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-droprule.html)
 --  remove a rewrite rule

[DROP SEQUENCE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-dropsequence.html)
 --  remove a sequence

[DROP TABLE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-droptable.html)
 --  remove a table

[DROP TRIGGER](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-droptrigger.html)
 --  remove a trigger

[DROP TYPE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-droptype.html)
 --  remove a user-defined data type

[DROP USER](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-dropuser.html)
 --  remove a database user account

[DROP VIEW](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-dropview.html)
 --  remove a view

[END](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-end.html)
 --  commit the current transaction

[EXPLAIN](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-explain.html)
 --  show the execution plan of a statement

[FETCH](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-fetch.html)
 --  retrieve rows from a table using a cursor

[GRANT](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-grant.html)
 -- define access privileges

[INSERT](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-insert.html)
 --  create new rows in a table

[LISTEN](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-listen.html)
 --  listen for a notification

[LOAD](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-load.html)
 -- load or reload a shared library file

[LOCK](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-lock.html)
 --  explicitly lock a table

[MOVE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-move.html)
 --  position a cursor on a specified row of a table

[NOTIFY](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-notify.html)
 --  generate a notification

[REINDEX](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-reindex.html)
 --  rebuild corrupted indexes

[RESET](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-reset.html)
 -- restore the value of a run-time parameter to a default value

[REVOKE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-revoke.html)
 -- remove access privileges

[ROLLBACK](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-rollback.html)
 --  abort the current transaction

[SELECT](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-select.html)
 --  retrieve rows from a table or view

[SELECT INTO](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-selectinto.html)
 --  create a new table from the results of a query

[SET](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-set.html)
 -- change a run-time parameter

[SET CONSTRAINTS](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-set-constraints.html)
 -- set the constraint mode of the current transaction

[SET SESSION AUTHORIZATION](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-set-session-authorization.html)
 -- set the session user identifier and the current user identifier of the current session

[SET TRANSACTION](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-set-transaction.html)
 -- set the characteristics of the current transaction

[SHOW](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-show.html)
 -- show the value of a run-time parameter

[TRUNCATE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-truncate.html)
 --  empty a table

[UNLISTEN](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-unlisten.html)
 --  stop listening for a notification

[UPDATE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-update.html)
 --  update rows of a table

[VACUUM](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/7.2/sql-vacuum.html)
 --  garbage-collect and optionally analyze a database

50.74. [pg\_seclabels](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.6/view-pg-seclabels.html)

50.75. [pg\_settings](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.6/view-pg-settings.html)

50.76. [pg\_shadow](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.6/view-pg-shadow.html)

50.77. [pg\_stats](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.6/view-pg-stats.html)

50.78. [pg\_tables](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.6/view-pg-tables.html)

50.79. [pg\_timezone\_abbrevs](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.6/view-pg-timezone-abbrevs.html)

50.80. [pg\_timezone\_names](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.6/view-pg-timezone-names.html)

50.81. [pg\_user](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.6/view-pg-user.html)

50.82. [pg\_user\_mappings](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.6/view-pg-user-mappings.html)

50.83. [pg\_views](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.6/view-pg-views.html)

The system catalogs are the place where a relational database management system stores schema metadata, such as information about tables and columns, and internal bookkeeping information. PostgreSQL's system catalogs are regular tables. You can drop and recreate the tables, add columns, insert and update values, and severely mess up your system that way. Normally, one should not change the system catalogs by hand, there are normally SQL commands to do that. (For example, CREATE DATABASE inserts a row into the pg\_database catalog — and actually creates the database on disk.) There are some exceptions for particularly esoteric operations, but many of those have been made available as SQL commands over time, and so the need for direct manipulation of the system catalogs is ever decreasing.

# PostgreSQL: Documentation: 8.2: SQL Commands

September 25, 2025: [PostgreSQL 18 Released!](https://www.postgresql.org/about/news/postgresql-18-released-3142/)

[Documentation](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/ "Documentation")
 → [PostgreSQL 8.2](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/index.html)

Supported Versions: [Current](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/sql-commands.html "PostgreSQL 18 - SQL Commands")
 ([18](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/18/sql-commands.html "PostgreSQL 18 - SQL Commands")
) / [17](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/17/sql-commands.html "PostgreSQL 17 - SQL Commands")
 / [16](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/16/sql-commands.html "PostgreSQL 16 - SQL Commands")
 / [15](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/15/sql-commands.html "PostgreSQL 15 - SQL Commands")

 / [13](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/13/sql-commands.html "PostgreSQL 13 - SQL Commands")

Development Versions: [devel](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/devel/sql-commands.html "PostgreSQL devel - SQL Commands")

Unsupported versions: [12](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/12/sql-commands.html "PostgreSQL 12 - SQL Commands")
 / [11](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-commands.html "PostgreSQL 11 - SQL Commands")
 / [10](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/10/sql-commands.html "PostgreSQL 10 - SQL Commands")
 / [9.6](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.6/sql-commands.html "PostgreSQL 9.6 - SQL Commands")

I. SQL Commands
===============

This part contains reference information for the SQL commands supported by PostgreSQL. By "SQL" the language in general is meant; information about the standards conformance and compatibility of each command can be found on the respective reference page.

Table of Contents

[ABORT](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-abort.html)
 -- abort the current transaction

[ALTER AGGREGATE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-alteraggregate.html)
 -- change the definition of an aggregate function

[ALTER CONVERSION](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-alterconversion.html)
 -- change the definition of a conversion

[ALTER DATABASE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-alterdatabase.html)
 -- change a database

[ALTER DOMAIN](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-alterdomain.html)
 --  change the definition of a domain

[ALTER FUNCTION](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-alterfunction.html)
 -- change the definition of a function

[ALTER GROUP](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-altergroup.html)
 -- change role name or membership

[ALTER INDEX](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-alterindex.html)
 -- change the definition of an index

[ALTER LANGUAGE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-alterlanguage.html)
 -- change the definition of a procedural language

[ALTER OPERATOR](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-alteroperator.html)
 -- change the definition of an operator

[ALTER OPERATOR CLASS](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-alteropclass.html)
 -- change the definition of an operator class

[ALTER ROLE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-alterrole.html)
 -- change a database role

[ALTER SCHEMA](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-alterschema.html)
 -- change the definition of a schema

[ALTER SEQUENCE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-altersequence.html)
 --  change the definition of a sequence generator

[ALTER TABLE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-altertable.html)
 -- change the definition of a table

[ALTER TABLESPACE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-altertablespace.html)
 -- change the definition of a tablespace

[ALTER TRIGGER](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-altertrigger.html)
 -- change the definition of a trigger

[ALTER TYPE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-altertype.html)
 --  change the definition of a type

[ALTER USER](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-alteruser.html)
 -- change a database role

[ANALYZE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-analyze.html)
 -- collect statistics about a database

[BEGIN](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-begin.html)
 -- start a transaction block

[CHECKPOINT](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-checkpoint.html)
 -- force a transaction log checkpoint

[CLOSE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-close.html)
 -- close a cursor

[CLUSTER](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-cluster.html)
 -- cluster a table according to an index

[COMMENT](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-comment.html)
 -- define or change the comment of an object

[COMMIT](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-commit.html)
 -- commit the current transaction

[COMMIT PREPARED](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-commit-prepared.html)
 -- commit a transaction that was earlier prepared for two-phase commit

[COPY](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-copy.html)
 -- copy data between a file and a table

[CREATE AGGREGATE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-createaggregate.html)
 -- define a new aggregate function

[CREATE CAST](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-createcast.html)
 -- define a new cast

[CREATE CONSTRAINT TRIGGER](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-createconstraint.html)
 -- define a new constraint trigger

[CREATE CONVERSION](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-createconversion.html)
 -- define a new encoding conversion

[CREATE DATABASE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-createdatabase.html)
 -- create a new database

[CREATE DOMAIN](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-createdomain.html)
 -- define a new domain

[CREATE FUNCTION](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-createfunction.html)
 -- define a new function

[CREATE GROUP](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-creategroup.html)
 -- define a new database role

[CREATE INDEX](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-createindex.html)
 -- define a new index

[CREATE LANGUAGE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-createlanguage.html)
 -- define a new procedural language

[CREATE OPERATOR](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-createoperator.html)
 -- define a new operator

[CREATE OPERATOR CLASS](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-createopclass.html)
 -- define a new operator class

[CREATE ROLE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-createrole.html)
 -- define a new database role

[CREATE RULE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-createrule.html)
 -- define a new rewrite rule

[CREATE SCHEMA](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-createschema.html)
 -- define a new schema

[CREATE SEQUENCE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-createsequence.html)
 -- define a new sequence generator

[CREATE TABLE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-createtable.html)
 -- define a new table

[CREATE TABLE AS](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-createtableas.html)
 -- define a new table from the results of a query

[CREATE TABLESPACE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-createtablespace.html)
 -- define a new tablespace

[CREATE TRIGGER](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-createtrigger.html)
 -- define a new trigger

[CREATE TYPE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-createtype.html)
 -- define a new data type

[CREATE USER](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-createuser.html)
 -- define a new database role

[CREATE VIEW](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-createview.html)
 -- define a new view

[DEALLOCATE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-deallocate.html)
 -- deallocate a prepared statement

[DECLARE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-declare.html)
 -- define a cursor

[DELETE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-delete.html)
 -- delete rows of a table

[DROP AGGREGATE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-dropaggregate.html)
 -- remove an aggregate function

[DROP CAST](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-dropcast.html)
 -- remove a cast

[DROP CONVERSION](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-dropconversion.html)
 -- remove a conversion

[DROP DATABASE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-dropdatabase.html)
 -- remove a database

[DROP DOMAIN](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-dropdomain.html)
 -- remove a domain

[DROP FUNCTION](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-dropfunction.html)
 -- remove a function

[DROP GROUP](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-dropgroup.html)
 -- remove a database role

[DROP INDEX](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-dropindex.html)
 -- remove an index

[DROP LANGUAGE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-droplanguage.html)
 -- remove a procedural language

[DROP OPERATOR](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-dropoperator.html)
 -- remove an operator

[DROP OPERATOR CLASS](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-dropopclass.html)
 -- remove an operator class

[DROP OWNED](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-drop-owned.html)
 -- remove database objects owned by a database role

[DROP ROLE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-droprole.html)
 -- remove a database role

[DROP RULE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-droprule.html)
 -- remove a rewrite rule

[DROP SCHEMA](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-dropschema.html)
 -- remove a schema

[DROP SEQUENCE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-dropsequence.html)
 -- remove a sequence

[DROP TABLE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-droptable.html)
 -- remove a table

[DROP TABLESPACE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-droptablespace.html)
 -- remove a tablespace

[DROP TRIGGER](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-droptrigger.html)
 -- remove a trigger

[DROP TYPE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-droptype.html)
 -- remove a data type

[DROP USER](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-dropuser.html)
 -- remove a database role

[DROP VIEW](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-dropview.html)
 -- remove a view

[END](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-end.html)
 -- commit the current transaction

[EXECUTE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-execute.html)
 -- execute a prepared statement

[EXPLAIN](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-explain.html)
 -- show the execution plan of a statement

[FETCH](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-fetch.html)
 -- retrieve rows from a query using a cursor

[GRANT](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-grant.html)
 -- define access privileges

[INSERT](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-insert.html)
 -- create new rows in a table

[LISTEN](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-listen.html)
 -- listen for a notification

[LOAD](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-load.html)
 -- load a shared library file

[LOCK](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-lock.html)
 -- lock a table

[MOVE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-move.html)
 -- position a cursor

[NOTIFY](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-notify.html)
 -- generate a notification

[PREPARE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-prepare.html)
 -- prepare a statement for execution

[PREPARE TRANSACTION](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-prepare-transaction.html)
 -- prepare the current transaction for two-phase commit

[REASSIGN OWNED](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-reassign-owned.html)
 -- change the ownership of database objects owned by a database role

[REINDEX](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-reindex.html)
 -- rebuild indexes

[RELEASE SAVEPOINT](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-release-savepoint.html)
 -- destroy a previously defined savepoint

[RESET](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-reset.html)
 -- restore the value of a run-time parameter to the default value

[REVOKE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-revoke.html)
 -- remove access privileges

[ROLLBACK](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-rollback.html)
 -- abort the current transaction

[ROLLBACK PREPARED](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-rollback-prepared.html)
 -- cancel a transaction that was earlier prepared for two-phase commit

[ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-rollback-to.html)
 -- roll back to a savepoint

[SAVEPOINT](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-savepoint.html)
 -- define a new savepoint within the current transaction

[SELECT](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-select.html)
 -- retrieve rows from a table or view

[SELECT INTO](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-selectinto.html)
 -- define a new table from the results of a query

[SET](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-set.html)
 -- change a run-time parameter

[SET CONSTRAINTS](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-set-constraints.html)
 -- set constraint checking modes for the current transaction

[SET ROLE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-set-role.html)
 -- set the current user identifier of the current session

[SET SESSION AUTHORIZATION](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-set-session-authorization.html)
 -- set the session user identifier and the current user identifier of the current session

[SET TRANSACTION](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-set-transaction.html)
 -- set the characteristics of the current transaction

[SHOW](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-show.html)
 -- show the value of a run-time parameter

[START TRANSACTION](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-start-transaction.html)
 -- start a transaction block

[TRUNCATE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-truncate.html)
 -- empty a table or set of tables

[UNLISTEN](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-unlisten.html)
 -- stop listening for a notification

[UPDATE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-update.html)
 -- update rows of a table

[VACUUM](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-vacuum.html)
 -- garbage-collect and optionally analyze a database

[VALUES](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/8.2/sql-values.html)
 -- compute a set of rows

* * *

* * *

Part III. Server Administration
===============================

This part covers topics that are of interest to a PostgreSQL database administrator. This includes installation of the software, set up and configuration of the server, management of users and databases, and maintenance tasks. Anyone who runs a PostgreSQL server, even for personal use, but especially in production, should be familiar with the topics covered in this part.

The information in this part is arranged approximately in the order in which a new user should read it. But the chapters are self-contained and can be read individually as desired. The information in this part is presented in a narrative fashion in topical units. Readers looking for a complete description of a particular command should see [Part VI](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/reference.html "Part VI. Reference")
.

The first few chapters are written so they can be understood without prerequisite knowledge, so new users who need to set up their own server can begin their exploration with this part. The rest of this part is about tuning and management; that material assumes that the reader is familiar with the general use of the PostgreSQL database system. Readers are encouraged to look at [Part I](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/tutorial.html "Part I. Tutorial")
 and [Part II](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql.html "Part II. The SQL Language")
 for additional information.

* * *

SQL Commands
============

* * *

This part contains reference information for the SQL commands supported by PostgreSQL. By “SQL” the language in general is meant; information about the standards conformance and compatibility of each command can be found on the respective reference page.

**Table of Contents**

[ABORT](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-abort.html)
 — abort the current transaction

[ALTER AGGREGATE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-alteraggregate.html)
 — change the definition of an aggregate function

[ALTER COLLATION](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-altercollation.html)
 — change the definition of a collation

[ALTER CONVERSION](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-alterconversion.html)
 — change the definition of a conversion

[ALTER DATABASE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-alterdatabase.html)
 — change a database

[ALTER DEFAULT PRIVILEGES](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-alterdefaultprivileges.html)
 — define default access privileges

[ALTER DOMAIN](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-alterdomain.html)
 — change the definition of a domain

[ALTER EVENT TRIGGER](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-altereventtrigger.html)
 — change the definition of an event trigger

[ALTER EXTENSION](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-alterextension.html)
 — change the definition of an extension

[ALTER FOREIGN DATA WRAPPER](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-alterforeigndatawrapper.html)
 — change the definition of a foreign-data wrapper

[ALTER FOREIGN TABLE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-alterforeigntable.html)
 — change the definition of a foreign table

[ALTER FUNCTION](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-alterfunction.html)
 — change the definition of a function

[ALTER GROUP](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-altergroup.html)
 — change role name or membership

[ALTER INDEX](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-alterindex.html)
 — change the definition of an index

[ALTER LANGUAGE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-alterlanguage.html)
 — change the definition of a procedural language

[ALTER LARGE OBJECT](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-alterlargeobject.html)
 — change the definition of a large object

[ALTER MATERIALIZED VIEW](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-altermaterializedview.html)
 — change the definition of a materialized view

[ALTER OPERATOR](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-alteroperator.html)
 — change the definition of an operator

[ALTER OPERATOR CLASS](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-alteropclass.html)
 — change the definition of an operator class

[ALTER OPERATOR FAMILY](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-alteropfamily.html)
 — change the definition of an operator family

[ALTER POLICY](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-alterpolicy.html)
 — change the definition of a row level security policy

[ALTER PROCEDURE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-alterprocedure.html)
 — change the definition of a procedure

[ALTER PUBLICATION](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-alterpublication.html)
 — change the definition of a publication

[ALTER ROLE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-alterrole.html)
 — change a database role

[ALTER ROUTINE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-alterroutine.html)
 — change the definition of a routine

[ALTER RULE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-alterrule.html)
 — change the definition of a rule

[ALTER SCHEMA](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-alterschema.html)
 — change the definition of a schema

[ALTER SEQUENCE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-altersequence.html)
 — change the definition of a sequence generator

[ALTER SERVER](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-alterserver.html)
 — change the definition of a foreign server

[ALTER STATISTICS](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-alterstatistics.html)
 — change the definition of an extended statistics object

[ALTER SUBSCRIPTION](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-altersubscription.html)
 — change the definition of a subscription

[ALTER SYSTEM](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-altersystem.html)
 — change a server configuration parameter

[ALTER TABLE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-altertable.html)
 — change the definition of a table

[ALTER TABLESPACE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-altertablespace.html)
 — change the definition of a tablespace

[ALTER TEXT SEARCH CONFIGURATION](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-altertsconfig.html)
 — change the definition of a text search configuration

[ALTER TEXT SEARCH DICTIONARY](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-altertsdictionary.html)
 — change the definition of a text search dictionary

[ALTER TEXT SEARCH PARSER](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-altertsparser.html)
 — change the definition of a text search parser

[ALTER TEXT SEARCH TEMPLATE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-altertstemplate.html)
 — change the definition of a text search template

[ALTER TRIGGER](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-altertrigger.html)
 — change the definition of a trigger

[ALTER TYPE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-altertype.html)
 — change the definition of a type

[ALTER USER](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-alteruser.html)
 — change a database role

[ALTER USER MAPPING](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-alterusermapping.html)
 — change the definition of a user mapping

[ALTER VIEW](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-alterview.html)
 — change the definition of a view

[ANALYZE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-analyze.html)
 — collect statistics about a database

[BEGIN](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-begin.html)
 — start a transaction block

[CALL](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-call.html)
 — invoke a procedure

[CHECKPOINT](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-checkpoint.html)
 — force a write-ahead log checkpoint

[CLOSE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-close.html)
 — close a cursor

[CLUSTER](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-cluster.html)
 — cluster a table according to an index

[COMMENT](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-comment.html)
 — define or change the comment of an object

[COMMIT](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-commit.html)
 — commit the current transaction

[COMMIT PREPARED](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-commit-prepared.html)
 — commit a transaction that was earlier prepared for two-phase commit

[COPY](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-copy.html)
 — copy data between a file and a table

[CREATE ACCESS METHOD](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-create-access-method.html)
 — define a new access method

[CREATE AGGREGATE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-createaggregate.html)
 — define a new aggregate function

[CREATE CAST](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-createcast.html)
 — define a new cast

[CREATE COLLATION](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-createcollation.html)
 — define a new collation

[CREATE CONVERSION](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-createconversion.html)
 — define a new encoding conversion

[CREATE DATABASE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-createdatabase.html)
 — create a new database

[CREATE DOMAIN](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-createdomain.html)
 — define a new domain

[CREATE EVENT TRIGGER](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-createeventtrigger.html)
 — define a new event trigger

[CREATE EXTENSION](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-createextension.html)
 — install an extension

[CREATE FOREIGN DATA WRAPPER](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-createforeigndatawrapper.html)
 — define a new foreign-data wrapper

[CREATE FOREIGN TABLE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-createforeigntable.html)
 — define a new foreign table

[CREATE FUNCTION](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-createfunction.html)
 — define a new function

[CREATE GROUP](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-creategroup.html)
 — define a new database role

[CREATE INDEX](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-createindex.html)
 — define a new index

[CREATE LANGUAGE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-createlanguage.html)
 — define a new procedural language

[CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-creatematerializedview.html)
 — define a new materialized view

[CREATE OPERATOR](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-createoperator.html)
 — define a new operator

[CREATE OPERATOR CLASS](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-createopclass.html)
 — define a new operator class

[CREATE OPERATOR FAMILY](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-createopfamily.html)
 — define a new operator family

[CREATE POLICY](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-createpolicy.html)
 — define a new row level security policy for a table

[CREATE PROCEDURE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-createprocedure.html)
 — define a new procedure

[CREATE PUBLICATION](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-createpublication.html)
 — define a new publication

[CREATE ROLE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-createrole.html)
 — define a new database role

[CREATE RULE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-createrule.html)
 — define a new rewrite rule

[CREATE SCHEMA](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-createschema.html)
 — define a new schema

[CREATE SEQUENCE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-createsequence.html)
 — define a new sequence generator

[CREATE SERVER](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-createserver.html)
 — define a new foreign server

[CREATE STATISTICS](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-createstatistics.html)
 — define extended statistics

[CREATE SUBSCRIPTION](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-createsubscription.html)
 — define a new subscription

[CREATE TABLE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-createtable.html)
 — define a new table

[CREATE TABLE AS](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-createtableas.html)
 — define a new table from the results of a query

[CREATE TABLESPACE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-createtablespace.html)
 — define a new tablespace

[CREATE TEXT SEARCH CONFIGURATION](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-createtsconfig.html)
 — define a new text search configuration

[CREATE TEXT SEARCH DICTIONARY](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-createtsdictionary.html)
 — define a new text search dictionary

[CREATE TEXT SEARCH PARSER](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-createtsparser.html)
 — define a new text search parser

[CREATE TEXT SEARCH TEMPLATE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-createtstemplate.html)
 — define a new text search template

[CREATE TRANSFORM](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-createtransform.html)
 — define a new transform

[CREATE TRIGGER](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-createtrigger.html)
 — define a new trigger

[CREATE TYPE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-createtype.html)
 — define a new data type

[CREATE USER](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-createuser.html)
 — define a new database role

[CREATE USER MAPPING](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-createusermapping.html)
 — define a new mapping of a user to a foreign server

[CREATE VIEW](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-createview.html)
 — define a new view

[DEALLOCATE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-deallocate.html)
 — deallocate a prepared statement

[DECLARE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-declare.html)
 — define a cursor

[DELETE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-delete.html)
 — delete rows of a table

[DISCARD](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-discard.html)
 — discard session state

[DO](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-do.html)
 — execute an anonymous code block

[DROP ACCESS METHOD](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-drop-access-method.html)
 — remove an access method

[DROP AGGREGATE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-dropaggregate.html)
 — remove an aggregate function

[DROP CAST](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-dropcast.html)
 — remove a cast

[DROP COLLATION](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-dropcollation.html)
 — remove a collation

[DROP CONVERSION](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-dropconversion.html)
 — remove a conversion

[DROP DATABASE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-dropdatabase.html)
 — remove a database

[DROP DOMAIN](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-dropdomain.html)
 — remove a domain

[DROP EVENT TRIGGER](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-dropeventtrigger.html)
 — remove an event trigger

[DROP EXTENSION](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-dropextension.html)
 — remove an extension

[DROP FOREIGN DATA WRAPPER](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-dropforeigndatawrapper.html)
 — remove a foreign-data wrapper

[DROP FOREIGN TABLE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-dropforeigntable.html)
 — remove a foreign table

[DROP FUNCTION](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-dropfunction.html)
 — remove a function

[DROP GROUP](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-dropgroup.html)
 — remove a database role

[DROP INDEX](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-dropindex.html)
 — remove an index

[DROP LANGUAGE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-droplanguage.html)
 — remove a procedural language

[DROP MATERIALIZED VIEW](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-dropmaterializedview.html)
 — remove a materialized view

[DROP OPERATOR](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-dropoperator.html)
 — remove an operator

[DROP OPERATOR CLASS](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-dropopclass.html)
 — remove an operator class

[DROP OPERATOR FAMILY](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-dropopfamily.html)
 — remove an operator family

[DROP OWNED](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-drop-owned.html)
 — remove database objects owned by a database role

[DROP POLICY](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-droppolicy.html)
 — remove a row level security policy from a table

[DROP PROCEDURE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-dropprocedure.html)
 — remove a procedure

[DROP PUBLICATION](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-droppublication.html)
 — remove a publication

[DROP ROLE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-droprole.html)
 — remove a database role

[DROP ROUTINE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-droproutine.html)
 — remove a routine

[DROP RULE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-droprule.html)
 — remove a rewrite rule

[DROP SCHEMA](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-dropschema.html)
 — remove a schema

[DROP SEQUENCE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-dropsequence.html)
 — remove a sequence

[DROP SERVER](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-dropserver.html)
 — remove a foreign server descriptor

[DROP STATISTICS](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-dropstatistics.html)
 — remove extended statistics

[DROP SUBSCRIPTION](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-dropsubscription.html)
 — remove a subscription

[DROP TABLE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-droptable.html)
 — remove a table

[DROP TABLESPACE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-droptablespace.html)
 — remove a tablespace

[DROP TEXT SEARCH CONFIGURATION](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-droptsconfig.html)
 — remove a text search configuration

[DROP TEXT SEARCH DICTIONARY](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-droptsdictionary.html)
 — remove a text search dictionary

[DROP TEXT SEARCH PARSER](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-droptsparser.html)
 — remove a text search parser

[DROP TEXT SEARCH TEMPLATE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-droptstemplate.html)
 — remove a text search template

[DROP TRANSFORM](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-droptransform.html)
 — remove a transform

[DROP TRIGGER](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-droptrigger.html)
 — remove a trigger

[DROP TYPE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-droptype.html)
 — remove a data type

[DROP USER](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-dropuser.html)
 — remove a database role

[DROP USER MAPPING](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-dropusermapping.html)
 — remove a user mapping for a foreign server

[DROP VIEW](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-dropview.html)
 — remove a view

[END](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-end.html)
 — commit the current transaction

[EXECUTE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-execute.html)
 — execute a prepared statement

[EXPLAIN](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-explain.html)
 — show the execution plan of a statement

[FETCH](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-fetch.html)
 — retrieve rows from a query using a cursor

[GRANT](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-grant.html)
 — define access privileges

[IMPORT FOREIGN SCHEMA](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-importforeignschema.html)
 — import table definitions from a foreign server

[INSERT](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-insert.html)
 — create new rows in a table

[LISTEN](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-listen.html)
 — listen for a notification

[LOAD](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-load.html)
 — load a shared library file

[LOCK](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-lock.html)
 — lock a table

[MOVE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-move.html)
 — position a cursor

[NOTIFY](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-notify.html)
 — generate a notification

[PREPARE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-prepare.html)
 — prepare a statement for execution

[PREPARE TRANSACTION](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-prepare-transaction.html)
 — prepare the current transaction for two-phase commit

[REASSIGN OWNED](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-reassign-owned.html)
 — change the ownership of database objects owned by a database role

[REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-refreshmaterializedview.html)
 — replace the contents of a materialized view

[REINDEX](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-reindex.html)
 — rebuild indexes

[RELEASE SAVEPOINT](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-release-savepoint.html)
 — destroy a previously defined savepoint

[RESET](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-reset.html)
 — restore the value of a run-time parameter to the default value

[REVOKE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-revoke.html)
 — remove access privileges

[ROLLBACK](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-rollback.html)
 — abort the current transaction

[ROLLBACK PREPARED](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-rollback-prepared.html)
 — cancel a transaction that was earlier prepared for two-phase commit

[ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-rollback-to.html)
 — roll back to a savepoint

[SAVEPOINT](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-savepoint.html)
 — define a new savepoint within the current transaction

[SECURITY LABEL](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-security-label.html)
 — define or change a security label applied to an object

[SELECT](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-select.html)
 — retrieve rows from a table or view

[SELECT INTO](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-selectinto.html)
 — define a new table from the results of a query

[SET](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-set.html)
 — change a run-time parameter

[SET CONSTRAINTS](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-set-constraints.html)
 — set constraint check timing for the current transaction

[SET ROLE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-set-role.html)
 — set the current user identifier of the current session

[SET SESSION AUTHORIZATION](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-set-session-authorization.html)
 — set the session user identifier and the current user identifier of the current session

[SET TRANSACTION](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-set-transaction.html)
 — set the characteristics of the current transaction

[SHOW](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-show.html)
 — show the value of a run-time parameter

[START TRANSACTION](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-start-transaction.html)
 — start a transaction block

[TRUNCATE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-truncate.html)
 — empty a table or set of tables

[UNLISTEN](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-unlisten.html)
 — stop listening for a notification

[UPDATE](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-update.html)
 — update rows of a table

[VACUUM](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-vacuum.html)
 — garbage-collect and optionally analyze a database

[VALUES](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/sql-values.html)
 — compute a set of rows
